speaker: interviewer
text: And first the general. So Peter, we were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me, don't I have to plug this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yay yay. He doesn't his-- Peter, we were talking about Alexander [INAUDIBLE] and how he helped your mother and sister. Where were your mother and sister staying when he went to get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In with nuns, with nuns. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a Catholic-- how do you call it? Sanctuary or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, where you were staying then.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was staying in a boy's school where just only boys were. And my mother and-- and my sister were staying with-- with nuns where only girls were, OK, in a Catholic school, but only girls were there. And Alexander found out that next day the Nazi's going to go up and execute everybody. And so-- so he went up and brought my sister and mother down, and so they escaped, and they survived. And he took them to a place-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the Buda part [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of Budapest into an apartment [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they were able to hide out. So that's-- that's how that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that he was working for the Resistance. How did you know he was working for the Resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me so. He told me so.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at the same time he was part of this Arrow Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: That how would I know that he wasn't a fascist really?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, he was Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I knew some Jewish people, very intelligent, and they didn't look Jewish. And they did similar things. They somehow were able to get uniforms, and they were able to-- and forged papers. And they were walking around like German officers and stuff like that. Very few of them. So I mean, if you're asking me do I have positive proof that he wasn't a real Arrow Cross person, I would have to say no, you know, but that would be--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, and he married my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. It wasn't a good marriage and they divorced. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, his whole family was Jewish and everything. There were some Jewish people who sold out themselves to the Germans, called kapos in-- and that was in-- mostly in camps like Buchenwald, Auschwitz, these kind of places where the Germans gave a little bit more food, a little bit better clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody that-- did you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: For the kapos or anybody like-- no. I heard about these stories after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you tell me-- you mentioned that your father helped a lady called Hannah--

speaker: interviewee
text: Szenes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Szenes. Could you tell me a little more about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The interesting thing is that my sister was going to a school in Budapest called [INAUDIBLE]. It's a school-- high school. And Hannah Szenes was going to that school. But Hannah Szenes was eight years older than my sister. So when my sister entered the school, Hannah was ready to graduate. But as the year end, the whole school was having a year-end celebration, and the girls dancing and everything. It was a little show, you know, going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was also invited and my parents. And so we were standing there, and that's where I met Hannah Szenes. And she was a beautiful girl, and she was dancing there. And she had a great effect on me. I never talked to her, but I just have seen her. And then later on during the war, her father was a very well-known writer in Hungary, Szenes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later on in life and during the war, she wanted to escape to Israel, probably a year before the war ended. And she needed money and my father helped her, giving her money, to be able to escape from Hungary. And she gave us a book I wish I would have, which she signed, with love to us, that we helped her. And that was her father's book, what her father wrote, and it was her handwriting. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we didn't see her, but we were listening to a British radio, Hungarian broadcasting, which was against the law. And that's how we learned that Hannah made it to England but volunteered with the British to come back, fly back, over Hungary and be parachuted in the Hungarian territory and be a spy [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the allies, which she did. And she got captured. When she parachuted out, they spotted her, and they captured her. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Do you know where?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But I know the prison where she was kept exactly, because I went there. When I went back to Hungary, I put my hand on the walls where she was just to touch it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we have-- on the Buda side, we have a place, a little tunnel which goes-- goes through a small mountain. And when you go-- before you go into that little tunnel, to the right-hand side there was a prison there where she-- she was kept. It was very close to the Danube River. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so she was kept there and never for one second she gave in. And I found this out later on when I read a book about her life and how brave she was and outspoken. And she was never sentenced to death, because at that time the Russians were too close to Budapest to take over. And everybody was really afraid that they are going to have to-- they be counted and asked about the crimes-- the war crimes-- they committed. So nobody wanted to do anything. But one man, one captain, was so angry at Hannah, because Hannah just-- I mean, spitted at these Nazis and yelled and used harsh language and everything and was very brave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So just about a week before the Russians came in and took over, this captain ran into the prison and had no right or no papers from any judge to execute her. But she had her carried to the execution place and had her executed. And that's how she died. You saw that or you read that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I read that. But-- but I know from-- from the fact that she has a monument in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father helped her to escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And I think my father sent some-- some livestock to Israel to the kibbutz where she wanted to go. But I don't think she ever arrived there, because I don't think she went any farther than London. And I think she came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So meanwhile, you are in hiding, and your mother also. So what happened then? What's the time period first, the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother came down from this place, and then what happened, I wanted to be with my mother and sister. So we went-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] let me see how it happened. Oh, so my mother and my sister lived in this apartment on Buda part in Budapest, and then for some reason, I don't know why, but in that Catholic school we couldn't stay any longer where we stayed. And then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Alexander, who saved my sister's and mother's life, came for my rescue and took him-- took me into his apartment. And he was hiding me out in his apartment. But what happened, Alexander's mother would come to Alexander's apartment, not as his mother but as a lady-- as a cleaning lady. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so when she came as a cleaning lady, the manager-- the apartment manager-- was telling her that Alexander is hiding our Jews-- me. So that gave us a danger signal that there is problem here. So I had to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Alexander knew a place, a bakery, which took in people. And so he took me to that bakery where I stayed, I think, one night. There was terrible, terrible bomb-- air raid going on in this-- at night. And this whole building was going like an earthquake, like a 7.5 earthquake. The whole building was just shaking and going, and in this bakery, besides me, I was the only child there, there was about 25 forced labor people who-- who escaped from forced labor camps, adult Jewish people would be there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then when the bombing came to a point where this-- everybody saw that the building going to collapse, all of them threw their bodies on top of me to protect me from dying. And if anybody would have died, they would have been first. That was also a very touching experience in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were they doing that? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- I think, and I don't want to make a prejudice statement, but-- but it is a prejudice statement when I say said I think Jewish people are brought up that they protect their kids to the extremes and willing to give their lives and sacrifice their lives to their kids or other people's kids. And I think because it's a way we are brought up, I think-- I think that was a natural instinct in them that this is like almost like a duty. Like a German to kill a Jew was a-- a duty, and to these people, to save a life of a kid, they felt it was their duty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they didn't know you, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't, and I don't think they had to know. I mean, if you go on the street and it's raining and it's dark and you see a small kid sitting on the street there alone crying, don't you think it's your duty that you should go there and find out what is the problem and can you help, even you don't know that little kid? I mean, that's how I feel that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the building fall?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, but I thought about it that-- that-- I thought about it writing-- writing a small play about this, you know? And because it was a very, very touching and interesting experience. And many things I do not recall as what was going on, but-- but I could concentrate, and I could think on it, and it would make a very interesting statement. And but if I ever would write a play about this, then I would-- I would have everybody die at the end. And I wouldn't-- because then people would think that this whole thing was a happy ending kind of affair. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by you recall? What do you recall? What were your emotions at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this bakery or generally speaking?

speaker: interviewer
text: In this bakery.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I had recollection of people joking around and trying to forget. The adults were trying to kind of talk and everything like everything would be normal and Germans don't exist and these kind of things, you know, to bring themselves to a better mood and to relax and so on and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the bombing, after it happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the bombing was constantly going on. What I want you to know is that besides that we had to be afraid for our lives during the war, and for the past year almost or so, there were constant bombings in Hungary, just like in Germany. I mean, we're talking about 24-hour bombings with intervals for let's say there was four-hour bombing and two hours not bombing. And there was other three hours. And it was just constantly going and never ending. I mean, that's how we lived. We either could have died of the bombs or we could have been captured by the Germans and shot on the street. So when we were living in an apartment on the third floor or fourth floor, and there was a constant air raid going, we couldn't go down to the air raid shelter to save our life, because we would have been caught there for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So also at a certain time, Alexander got us phony papers where our name became [INAUDIBLE], our family name instead of Fischl became [INAUDIBLE], which is a Hungarian name. And so my name was [INAUDIBLE] Peter, and I don't know the town, what it says the town we came from and so on and so on. So we had phony papers that we are Catholics and so on and so on, and we are refugees from the-- from territory where the Russians already occupied in Hungary. But thank god nobody ever examined the papers, because we would have been really then-- you know, if they would have pressed us, then we would have been real trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: There are things I recall that we lived in this Buda apartment, and the Germans had street cars-- street cars by the hundreds lined up in our neighborhood. And it was full with ammunition. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day I would look out on the window, and I will see all these bombs are falling into the Danube River but like raining, like training. I mean, you can imagine that was thousands of bombs. And if any bomb would have fallen on the streetcar, which was lined up before our house, the whole neighborhood would have disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was another interesting experience in-- in our lives when the landlady where we stayed in her apartment, said, well, I can't take this any longer, because the law was in Hungary if you were hiding Jews, and the Germans were catching you-- that you're hiding Jews, you were executed with the Jews immediately on the street. And she had kids also. So she said we have to leave immediately. I mean, she had just a nervous breakdown, and we understood. She helped us a lot, and she was very nice. So immediately we had to go and walk. Luckily, five or six miles away. Uh, there was another apartment, some friends who moved out from it and also escaped from the Russians. But the apartment became empty, and we heard about it. So we were able to go and walk towards that place. So that was before Christmas. And so we walked out from this apartment building. And I don't know, it took us a very long time, because the air raids were so strong that many times we had to throw our bodies on the-- on the-- on the street, down, because we were thinking that we're going to get killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to cross a bridge. I remember that. So finally, I don't know how many hours it took or maybe took a day or whatever, we arrived to that new apartment where-- where we-- that was our new home now for hiding out. And somehow the word goes very fast, you know, people telling stuff when you don't have newspaper or radio or somehow one person talks to another, and the news goes very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we learned that after we left this building where we were on the fourth floor and-- and all the streetcars were full with ammunition, a bomb did hit the streetcar, and the whole neighborhood disappeared. It was leveled. So here's another interesting thing in my life story, so to speak, that when you wonder as why things happening sometimes to you in life, immediately you have no explanation. And just like when I had this urge to go to the movies, which finally saved my-- my mother and sister's life, here again, this lady broke down and that saved our lives, because on the fourth floor, we would have been buried with five trillion tons of, you know, bricks and the whole building. We would have been still under there. And so-- so-- but we survived that. So from the bakery, you went to this place, and then from this place you moved to the-- across the river.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But tell me, I want to go back to the bakery. Why did you say that if you were doing a play, you would be-- it would be a happy ending to have everybody dying. I didn't understand that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said it would not be a happy ending.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. I didn't understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had a friend of mine. Whose name was Jan Kadar, and I met him in 1940s. He was a young man then. And mater of fact, he helped me with my homework as a school student. And his hobby was, he bought himself an 8 millimeter camera, and on the weekends he would go out and make shots with this little 8 millimeter camera. And that was his hobby. And then the war came, and we got parted and separated and everything. And when I was in America, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many, many, many years later, I was reading magazine that from Czechoslovakia, Jan Kadar is-- is-- how would you say-- he was up for an Oscar award as a director for a motion picture made in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to myself, that must be Jan. So I called Prague Information immediately. And I found him, and that was him. So we got together with Jan. And-- but I just wonder how did he come into my conversation now? It's why I'm telling you this, I don't know. I was asking you about the people in the building, in the bakery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes. OK, thank you. Jan Kadar was a true artist. And nobody could buy him. And in America, as an Oscar-winning director, he got many offers, and he refused most of the offers he got. And he took one job where Mohammad Ali was-- was playing, and it called It was a television movie and-- and he made it-- Jan made it. And he directed it, Jan Kadar directed it. And he told me, he said, at the end of this movie, there are many black kids there hiding from the whites. And in the script it was that all the blacks survive, and Jan said I tore the script book up, and I finished it the way I wanted it. And all the black people were executed in this movie. And there is one little boy who kind of looking at you before he gets shot. And that's when the movie ends and gets stop. And as long as I live, I will remember that picture and that ending. And he said that would have been phony the other way. And naturally, he was never-- his pictures didn't bring in money, but he made statements.

speaker: interviewer
text: About discrimination, I guess, is your point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And so-- so I felt when I told you that in my bakery story I would bring in for a dramatic point of view and also to-- to make a statement that everybody would have been dead, it would give a much better understanding, a picture of that era. I was telling you a true story with a true ending that people survived, although I don't know what happened to the people later on in that building. They could have died. And the building could have, but-- but in my mind, it would have been-- so that's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, so you went to the apartment with your family across the river. So what happened then? That was in which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1944, probably November. Probably November. And also, besides the Germans, the Hungarian Arrow Cross, the air raids, there was a problem of what do you eat? What do you drink? And to make a point and make a story somewhat shorter is that we ate everything what we could put our hands on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had-- because Budapest was surrounded by the Russians, because the Russians wanted to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Russians wanted to make peace with the Germans and let them give up Budapest, but the-- but the Germans shot the delegation, the Russian delegations. So the Russians encircled Budapest and just bypassed it, and they said you can starve to death now.
